data.name: Johny Placide
data.description: Johny Placide’s career has been built on patience, resilience and authority earned the hard way. Developed at Le Havre, he worked his way through French football before becoming a trusted last line of defence for Bastia. Not flashy, but fiercely reliable, Placide is known for sharp reflexes, strong positioning and safe hands, qualities that earned him the nickname the Guardian. He commands his area vocally, excels under pressure, and reads the game with the calm of a veteran. Born in Montfermeil, France, to Haitian parents, he chose to represent their homeland, embracing his roots with pride. A mainstay since 2011 and often captain, Placide has guided younger teammates through demanding campaigns, becoming a pillar of stability during the historic qualification push. As the dream of 2026 became reality, he declared: “We’ll be there to enjoy it and make the people proud.”

data.name: Carlens Arcus
data.description: From the spirited streets of Port-au-Prince, where he enjoyed what he describes as “a very happy, very peaceful childhood”, to the roar of Concacaf Gold Cup finals, Arcus has worn challenges with pride, like badges. Fast, fearless in attack and possessing tremendous stamina, he embodies the modern full-back: defender first, creator when it counts, and competitor always. A pillar of the Haiti team since 2016, Arcus delivered the cross for the goal that sealed their first World Cup berth since 1974, ending a 51-year drought. “I always had two dreams in my life,” he said. “I’d already achieved the first, which was to sign a professional contract. The second has just come true.” Arcus first caught the eye in France with Auxerre, where his overlapping runs and consistency made him indispensable, before refining his game with Vitesse Arnhem in the Netherlands.

data.name: Keeto Thermoncy
data.description: When Thermoncy pulls on Haiti’s colours, he carries two identities in every stride. Raised in Switzerland but fiercely connected to his Haitian roots, the young defender once explained his bond with the national team: “When you look at Haiti’s history and all its people have endured for decades, wearing this shirt means far more than football.” It is the kind of reflection that reveals the maturity behind one of Haiti’s brightest young prospects. Forged in the disciplined academy corridors of Young Boys (but still to make a first-team appearance), Thermoncy is a modern centre-back: quick across the turf, commanding in the air and calm enough to launch attacks from deep. At over 1.80m (5ft 11in), he marries athletic power with elegant timing, gliding into tackles with the assurance of a seasoned professional. His senior Haiti debut in World Cup qualifying felt less like a first step than a statement. Now, Thermoncy already looks built to anchor Haiti’s next generation.

data.name: Ricardo Adé
data.description: They call him the Wall for a reason. Ricardo Adé doesn’t just defend; he commands the game from the back, snuffing out attacks before they even begin. Quick on his feet yet unbeatable in the air, he reads the pitch like a grandmaster, his fearless streak anchoring Haiti’s defence for years. Adé’s story is even more inspiring than his performances. Early in his career, he was left stranded in Thailand after a failed move, surviving on little more than bread and condensed milk for a week: an ordeal that forged the resilience behind the player. At Liga de Quito, his booming headers and calm distribution have turned tense matches into triumphs, helping him lift four domestic trophies. “Defending is about reading the game and being ready for the battle,” he says, and every duel proves it. Off the field, Adé carries the same quiet authority, representing Haitian identity with pride and professionalism wherever he goes.

data.name: Hannes Delcroix
data.description: Born in Haiti and raised in Belgium, Delcroix grew up between two football cultures, Caribbean spirit and European precision. At Anderlecht he polished his craft; at Burnley he learned the bruising art of survival – and the need for patience. “My characteristics are someone who’s calm, off the pitch and also on the pitch,” he says and, after moving from Waalwijk to Burnley in 2023, he had to be calm as he waited for his chance. Eventually he realised it would not come, and after he was left out of their squad list at the start of the season he sought “a club that would guarantee me plenty of playing time and an important role”. He found that at Lugano. His international debut followed in October and he swiftly became a regular starter. Quick across the turf and dominant in the air, Delcroix is the kind of centre-back who steps into midfield with calm authority before launching a 40-yard diagonal as if it were second nature. Coaches praise his timing; teammates talk about his voice: steady, composed, uncompromising.

data.name: Carl Sainté
data.description: In Haiti’s Grand-Goâve, long before he became used to stadium floodlights and the MLS spotlight, Sainté learned to play on dusty pitches where the ball bounced like a question. He answers those questions now with authority. Tall, elegant and deceptively quick across the turf, Sainté is a midfielder who covers ground effortlessly, wins headers with timing, and threads passes through traffic as if he can see moves before they happen. He helped Violette to a Haitian league title, lifted the MLS Next Pro crown with North Texas, and climbed into Major League Soccer with Dallas. For Haiti, he plays with pride stitched into every tackle, calm amid chaos, brave in the air. Teammates call him the Metronome, because when Sainté moves, the whole team finds its rhythm. “We didn’t just qualify, we carried the heartbeat of a nation on to the pitch,” he wrote after Haiti sealed their World Cup spot. “Haiti has been through so much, but we keep showing the world what resilience looks like.”

data.name: Derrick Etienne Jr.
data.description: There is something electric about Derrick Etienne Jr when he squares up a defender: a shimmy, a burst, and suddenly he’s streaking down the flank. Born in Virginia but raised on a diet of Haitian pride, he followed his father – also Derrick – into the national team and was in turn followed by his brother, Danielle. When he first committed to Haiti, he spoke with the eagerness of a player embracing more than just football: “I’m just excited to get started … I think I’ll have a chance to be part of something special.” It proved more than words, and became a mission. Fast, skilful and surprisingly strong in the air, Etienne thrives on big moments. With Columbus Crew he lifted the MLS Cup in 2020, one of several honors in an MLS journey that has been shaped by resilience and reinvention.

data.name: Martin Expérience
data.description: There’s something fitting about Martin Expérience’s surname. On the pitch, he plays with experience beyond his years. Born in France but proudly tied to his Haitian roots, he carved his path through the rugged lower divisions before earning his place with Nancy, where he is swapping obscurity for packed terraces and bigger stages, as part of the team that won promotion to Ligue 2 in 2025. When Haiti sealed their place at the 2026 World Cup, Expérience could hardly contain himself. “Playing in a World Cup is extraordinary, no matter whom we face,” he said – before the draw paired them with Brazil, the country of his childhood idol, Neymar. Since making his debut for Haiti in 2021 he has brought steel and stamina to the Grenadiers’ backline, a modern full-back shaped by hard miles, big dreams and a flag he wears with pride.

data.name: Duckens Nazon
data.description: Known for his pace, physicality and knack for finding the net on the biggest stages, Nazon certainly doesn’t drift through matches, he crashes into them. Raised in the Paris suburbs, he learned early that space has to be earned, not given. That edge followed him from Lorient’s academy to stadiums across continents – his current employers, the Tehran-based Esteghlal, are the 11th team of his senior career, which has included stints at Coventry, Oldham and St Mirren and periods in Bulgaria and India. He is known as the Beast, and not for nothing. Nazon has a habit of saving his best for the loudest nights: World Cup qualifiers, Concacaf battles, moments when nerves tighten. Before the decisive qualifier against Nicaragua, Nazon gave a speech in the dressing room. “Here in Haiti, some people only have a few dollars in their pockets. They have nothing else. They only have us,” he said. “If we don’t win, they have nothing left. Today, we have the chance to give them a smile. We can make them cry with joy. They are counting on our feet. Please, don’t let them down.” His words did the trick.

data.name: Jean-Ricner Bellegarde
data.description: If midfield were a chessboard, Jean Ricner Bellegarde would be the queen: fast, versatile and impossible to contain. Born in Colombes, in the suburbs of Paris, he honed his craft in the local youth systems. At Strasbourg, he earned a reputation for darting runs, sharp vision and fearless tackles, traits that carried him to Wolves in the Premier League. Nicknamed the Architect, Bellegarde thrives on dictating tempo, threading passes that seem impossible and sparking counterattacks from nothing. Switching allegiance to Haiti after representing France at youth level, he brought flair and composure to the national side, inspiring young players across the Caribbean. “He has a natural ability to accelerate and hurt the opponent,” his former Strasbourg coach Thierry Laurey once said, a line that captures perfectly the burst, intelligence, and unpredictability that define Bellegarde’s game.

data.name: Louicius Deedson
data.description: The first thing you notice about Deedson is the blur: by the time defenders react, he is already gone. From Haiti to the United States – where he originally moved aged 15 – via the windswept grounds of Denmark, for Deedson, every sprint feels personal, every goal a message home. He plays on the edge of chaos: skilful, always driving forward as if the pitch was tilting in his favour. For Haiti, he has become more than a threat: he’s a spark. He scored the opening goal in the game against Nicaragua that sealed his nation’s place at the World Cup, a strike loaded with history. Deedson isn’t defined by trophies yet, but by the feeling he leaves behind. “This is the best feeling in my career so far,” he said of that goal. “It’s my feeling, and then the fans’ feeling, and then my family feeling back home.” Off the pitch, he is quieter, grounded by his journey. Those close to him speak of a player deeply attached to Haiti, carrying its flag with pride wherever he goes like football is not just a career, but a responsibility.

data.name: Alexandre Pierre
data.description: In the cages of Aubervilliers, just north of Paris, Alexandre Pierre learned to fly. A son of Haitian heritage, he would hurl himself across pitches long before he had proper gloves, chasing a dream that stretched far beyond the périphérique. Today, at 1.91m (6ft 3in), he commands his box with authority. Quick off his line and blessed with cat-like reflexes that turn certain goals into gasps. Pierre’s path wound through Stade Lavallois, Angers and Strasbourg, where he signed his first professional deal in 2021, later sharpening his claws on loan at Annecy. For Haiti, he has become part of a new generation daring to think bigger, featuring in Concacaf competitions and World Cup qualifiers. Calm but fiercely competitive, Pierre plays like a man still diving on concrete. Every save personal, every clean sheet a promise kept.

data.name: Duke Lacroix
data.description: Before he was marauding down the flank for Haiti, Duke Lacroix was a student athlete at the University of Pennsylvania, balancing lectures – he got a degree in sociology, and followed it with a master’s in sports psychology – with dreams that stretched far beyond campus. Fast, fearless, and blessed with a sprinter’s engine, Lacroix plays left-back like a winger in disguise. He bombs forward, whips in crosses, wins his headers despite not being the tallest and recovers with impressive pace. Since committing to the Haiti national football team in 2023, Lacroix has worn the colours with pride, attacking every duel as if it were personal. Outside football he is involved in politics and social activism, as vice-president of United Black Players, president of the USL Players Association, the union representing players in the United Soccer League, and working for Onrise, which provides mental health support to athletes and others in high-pressure roles.

data.name: Garven Metusala
data.description: Born in Canada to a Haitian mother and a Congolese father, Metusala worked his way up the local youth scene before joining the Hamilton side Forge Football Club in 2021. There he won two Canadian Premier League titles before moving to the US with Colorado Springs Switchbacks in 2025. Talking to the club website this year, he explained that he started out with kung fu before moving on to other sports. "I played basketball for a few years and really enjoyed it but my family is a big soccer family. My dad played in the past and I guess he gave this to me." Missed out on the initial World Cup squad but was called up as a replacement for Leverton Pierre on the eve of the tournament.

data.name: Ruben Providence
data.description: On the pitches of Paris Saint-Germain’s academy, Providence was the kind of winger who turned training into theatre: stepovers, bursts of pace and defenders left chasing shadows. That instinct was shaped in his early days at the club but by the time he moved to Roma in 2019, the raw flair had found structure, shaped in one of Europe’s most demanding environments, where he picked up youth honours and a sharper tactical edge. Fast and skilful, Providence plays with the freedom of a street footballer and the discipline of a graduate from two European giants. His spell at Hartberg added steel to his silk, bringing goals, assists and a taste for the fight. Now at Almere City in the Dutch second flight, he plays with freedom and intent. In 2025 he chose to represent Haiti, a decision rooted in pride more than convenience. Providence isn’t just chasing a career; he’s chasing a story worth telling.

data.name: Lenny Joseph
data.description: Joseph has always played like he has somewhere to be. There is something of the street footballer about him: sharp, fearless and always playing as if the next touch might change everything. Raised in Paris and polished in the academy of Paris Saint-Germain, he grew up in a world where flair was currency and survival meant standing out. His rise through French football eventually led him to Budapest and Ferencvaros, where European nights and domestic success have sharpened his edge. Lightning over the first five yards, clever in the air despite his lean frame and skilful enough to unpick defenders in tight spaces, he carries that rare ability to make chaos look choreographed. Still only 25, he plays with a restless hunger: the kind of striker who seems to treat every run as a statement. For Haiti, Joseph represents more than promise: he looks like the next headline waiting to happen.

data.name: Danley Jean Jacques
data.description: On the bustling pitches of Haiti’s Petit-Goâve, Danley Jean Jacques tackled as if survival was on the line. Friends swear he hated defeat so much he replayed matches in his head before sleep. That fire carried him from Don Bosco’s domestic title-winners to the hard grind of Metz, and on to Philadelphia in 2024. Quick across the grass, powerful in the air and fearless in the duel, he plays like a storm at the heart of midfield. In France Jean Jacques bulldozed through games, helping Metz secure promotion to Ligue 1. For Haiti he has scored vital goals, fists clenched, eyes closed in gratitude, as the Grenadiers chased history. Five years after leaving Haiti for France he has not lost his love of Haitian culture and cuisine – his favourite is diri ak sos pwa, rice with a black bean puree. “That’s the best,” he says. “Especially when my mother cooks it.” Will feel particularly at home when Haiti play Brazil – the game will be played at Philadelphia Stadium, his home ground.

data.name: Wilson Isidor
data.description: Isidor was the boy in Rennes who chased football like it owed him something, darting between cars on makeshift alleyway pitches, celebrating imaginary goals. Tall and quick with a predator’s instinct, he is equally dangerous in the air and on the turn, blending force with surprising grace. His journey took him from Monaco’s youth ranks to trophies in Russia with Zenit St Petersburg, where he lifted league and cup silverware before sprinting into Sunderland’s attack. He had represented France through the age levels but in March, Haiti finally got its man, Isidor confirming his long-awaited decision on social media: “I’ve seen all your messages … you asked for me, you waited for me, and here I am.” He made his full debut against Iceland, and scored his first goal in the same game. He brings real pedigree to Haiti’s forward line, the promise not just of goals but the ignition of a nation’s World Cup dream.

data.name: Yassin Fortuné
data.description: There’s a story told at Arsenal about a teenage Fortuné leaving defenders chasing shadows, his pace impossible to cage. But away from the pitch, the story softens. Raised in the Paris suburbs in a tight-knit family, Fortuné remains deeply grounded, more likely to spend time with loved ones than chase headlines. Arsenal signed him from Lens at 16 but he never played for their first team, moving to Sion in Switzerland three years later. Last summer he joined the Portuguese second-tier side Vizela and within a month was a full international, having represented France at various youth levels. Fortuné is explosive, skilful and surprisingly strong in the air. Teammates often describe a quiet character, focused, someone who lets his football do the talking. Yet there’s a playful side too, characterised by music, dressing-room laughter and that unmistakable street-football edge that never quite left him.

data.name: Frantzdy Pierrot
data.description: When Pierrot launches himself into the air, time seems to pause. Then the net ripples. Nicknamed the Mountain, the Haiti striker makes headers feel inevitable, overpowering centre-backs in the air while surprising them on the turf with soft touches and clever layoffs. Born in Cap-Haïtien but brought up in Massachusetts, his journey to Europe was anything but straightforward, passing through American college soccer before he learned to thrive under pressure on bigger stages in Belgium, France, Israel and Greece. At AEK Athens he seemed set to become the reference point up front, the man teammates search for when matches tighten and crosses start flying. But injury pushed him out of the team towards the end of last year, and he moved on loan to Turkey’s Caykur Rizespor in February. Off the pitch Pierrot keeps a low profile. A regular for Haiti, and averaging an impressive 1.5 games per goal, he carries his country’s expectations with quiet pride, reflecting a personality that is more measured than flashy: focused, grounded and built around collective effort rather than individual spotlight.

data.name: Josué Casimir
data.description: On the sunlit pitches of Guadeloupe, Josué Casimir learned football the hard way. No space, no time, just instinct. He grew up in a household where the game was less a hobby than a language. By the time he reached Le Havre’s academy, he already played with defiance. Coaches quickly learned not to cage him. “Let him run,” one reportedly joked, and run he does. Casimir is electric: quick over five yards, fearless in the air though he is not particularly tall, and blessed with a winger’s swagger. He doesn’t just beat defenders; he teases them. He plays with a smile you can almost hear. But beyond the flair lies something deeper. He played youth football for Guadeloupe, for whom his brother, Stevenson, plays in defence, but choosing Haiti, his parents’ homeland, gave his career a different heartbeat.

data.name: Jean-Kévin Duverne
data.description: Jean‑Kévin Duverne’s story is one of quiet tenacity and hard‑earned respect. On a sun‑drenched pitch in French Guiana in 2024, when he lifted his arm skyward after clasping that first Haiti cap, there was a palpable thrill, not just another defender making his debut, but a Grenadier staking his claim at international level. The moment was the culmination of years in France’s unforgiving leagues, where he learned to mix his raw pace with unwavering defensive grit. This past season he has played in Belgium with Gent on loan from Nantes. This fearless, athletic defender is as comfortable marshaling the backline as he is sparking attacks from deep. Duverne has become a symbol of pride for Haitian fans, a guardian at the heart of their hopes and a story that’s still unfolding. Off the pitch, he’s quiet but intense, a steadfast presence whose story is still being written one fearless tackle at the time.

data.name: Josué Duverger
data.description: On a humid night during Haiti’s qualifying campaign, Josué Duverger stood alone beneath the floodlights, the noise rising, the stakes even higher. He was just 17 when he chose to represent Haiti, a teenager with gloves slightly too big and similarly outsized reserves of courage. Born in Montréal and honed in Portugal’s academies, Duverger blends Latin technique with Caribbean fire. Quick, brave in the air and spring-heeled in one-on-ones, he is the kind of goalkeeper who seems to grow taller as shots fly toward him. At club level he has posted impressive clean-sheet runs, including a 50% shut-out rate during a season in Portugal’s Campeonato. There have been bruising lessons, including a humiliating double-miskick own goal against Canada, the country of his birth and which he had represented at under-17 level, but Duverger’s story is not about mistakes. It is about response. Dive. Rise. Repeat.

data.name: Wilguens Paugain
data.description: Paugain’s CV reads like a movie script: born in extreme poverty in Thomazeau in Haiti he was adopted aged five by a family in rural France and was spotted by Nancy-Lorraine aged 12. However, he suffered a serious injury at 18 that derailed his career, forced him out of the game for eight months, cost him the chance of a professional contract and pushed him to work for a while delivering pizzas. But he fought back and proceeded to play his way through dressing rooms scattered across Cyprus, Latvia and Austria, each stop adding a new layer to his game, before reaching Belgium. Paugain is the kind of full-back who sprints first and thinks later, but his instinct is normally proved right. He mixes raw athleticism with a growing technical edge, surging forward as if the touchline were a runway. “This is an opportunity for the world to stop neglecting Haiti,” he says. “When people talk about the country it is natural disasters, political instability and gangs, rarely about anything positive. I hope the World Cup will bring respect back to our team and our country.”

data.name: Dominique Simon
data.description: Simon’s journey has been anything but ordinary. From France to Estonia, Georgia, Czechia and now Slovakia, Simon has built his career the hard way, gaining experience across Europe. There is something distinctly Parisian about the way he plays: calm under pressure, technically polished and always a step ahead. Formed in the demanding academy of Paris Saint-Germain, he learned early that survival in elite football depends as much on intelligence as flair. Quick across the ground, sharp in the tackle and surprisingly strong in the air, he mixes steel with subtlety, capable of breaking up play one moment and launching attacks with a single crisp pass the next. Simon embodies Haiti’s new-generation diaspora talent: cultured, resilient and quietly ambitious. The kind of midfielder coaches trust, teammates lean on and opponents rarely enjoy facing.

data.name: Woodensky Pierre
data.description: In the noise of Haiti’s Cité Soleil, where football is played on instinct and survival, Pierre learned early that hesitation is expensive. Friends recall a skinny kid chasing games from sunrise to streetlight, fearless in the air despite his size, already barking instructions like a veteran. Now at Real Hope FA, Pierre is a midfield anchor with edge and elegance: quick over short distances, and skilful enough to slip out of tight spaces before snapping into a tackle. He rose through Violette, collecting domestic honours and earning recognition as one of Haiti’s most reliable young enforcers.  “Work hard and believe in yourself,” he says, a mentality shaped by his upbringing. With his eyes on success at the 2026 World Cup, Pierre plays like a man on a mission, carrying not just the ball, but the hopes of where he came from.
